Thought for 37s

Read.


The attached set establishes a pretty clear current state:


The three V3_2 JSON files are now the authoritative working set: the technical spec is the authoritative framework definition, the template is the executable plan contract, and the instruction document is the AI-facing rulebook for filling the template. The session-purpose file explicitly frames the rest of the bundle around baseline work, blocker remediation, and A.3 reindexing on branch npp-v3_2-baseline.


NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES


The biggest current governance point is that v3.2 is no longer just “structured planning.” The instruction document now makes pattern-first and configuration-driven behavior mandatory for executable work: no blank pattern_id, no ad hoc implementation when a pattern is missing, mandatory PATTERN_CREATION before implementation if no approved pattern exists, and executable behavior must come from registered executors consuming approved spec/config artifacts. It also explicitly calls out configuration_driven_development, pattern_governance, and executor_registry as mandatory governance surfaces.


NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_INSTRUCTION…


The technical spec matches that shift. In the validation layer it now defines 27 total mandatory gates, including the six configuration-driven enforcement gates GATE-CFG-001 through GATE-CFG-006, and it lists validation guarantees for configuration-driven enforcement, executor integrity, and blocking implementation until missing pattern packages are resolved.


NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_TECHNICAL_S…


NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_TECHNICAL_S…


Operationally, the indexing toolchain is there to make these JSON artifacts patchable and navigable without brittle array-index edits. index_generator.py builds inventory.json, emits semantic indexes, uses JSON Pointer as the canonical locator type, adds a composite by_step_id map for PHASE/STEP, and validates the result with five indexing gates (IDX-GATE-01..05).


index_generator


The session-purpose file confirms the intended outputs: inventory.json, per-file semantic indexes, resolver behavior, namespace checks, cross-file integrity checks, and readiness evidence.


NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES


The specific indexing model is also important: not every array is treated the same. index_generator.py marks some arrays as index_eligible and binds them by semantic identity, while others stay structural and must be replaced as whole arrays. One explicit special case is execution_phases, which remains structural and inventory-only rather than ID-indexed.


index_generator


The blocker history matters because it explains why this bundle exists in its current form. The evidence set and session-purpose doc say four blockers were already remediated: duplicate gate cleanup, enhancement ID migration to ENH-*, keeping execution_phases as structural/inventory-only, and backfilling component_id values. The same file says the evidence bundle now proves both blocker remediation and semantic-index readiness.


NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES


There is also a useful “delta from older thinking” in the historical notes you attached. Earlier analysis files argued that the system was still constraint-driven rather than truly pattern-constrained/configuration-driven. Those are now historical context, not the current source of truth. The current instruction/spec pair shows that much of that gap has been closed at the planning/governance layer. The remaining question, if you want to push further, is not whether v3.2 has pattern-first governance — it does — but whether you want to force the same model deeper into implementation architecture, engine/spec separation, and executor design.


ChatGPT-Template Feature Valida…


ChatGPT-Configuration-Driven De…


Net: these files describe a post-remediation v3.2 planning system where the three JSON governance documents are authoritative, semantic-ID indexing is in place to support stable RFC-6902 targeting, and executable work is now intended to be fail-closed, pattern-first, and configuration-driven rather than ad hoc.


NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES


NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_INSTRUCTION…


index_generator


If you want the next step, I can now use this context to evaluate gaps, compare old claims against current v3.2 reality, or map where the JSONs still need patching.

Below is a comprehensive gap analysis of the v3.2 artifacts and a mapping of the patching work still needed in the JSON files. The citations reference the v3.2 instruction document and technical specification to show why each gap matters.


1. Gap evaluation


a. Missing or null fields in task_analysis
The template leaves almost all task_analysis fields blank: the original request, project name, ground‑truth level, quantified targets, methodology phases/parallel groups and success‑criteria lists are all empty. Classification fields (complexity, domain, quality), the inferred behaviour values (verification_level, test_coverage_min), the decision log and the pattern_resolution_outputs (pattern decision, existing match, new pattern required, executor family, config‑driven feasibility) are all null. The instruction document requires summarising the user request, classifying the domain and complexity, and recording a pattern resolution decision with the existing pattern match and whether a new pattern is needed. Without these values, the plan has no clear understanding of the task or the existence of a valid pattern.


b. Empty assumptions_scope and lack of constraints
The assumptions_scope arrays (assumptions, in_scope, out_of_scope, definition_of_done) are empty. The instruction document makes “NO IMPLIED BEHAVIOUR” part of the critical constraint and requires that explicit assumptions and scope boundaries be stated. Leaving them empty risks hidden expectations and violates the pattern‑first governance model.


c. file_manifest incomplete
The file_manifest.global_files array and its pattern‑related entries (entry_extensions.pattern_id, pattern_role) are empty. A plan should reference all spec/config artifacts used to implement patterns and govern execution, as mandated by configuration‑driven development.


d. Unfilled phase_plan
The phase_plan.phase_overview and parallel_execution_groups are empty, and the time_estimate values are null. The spec emphasises concurrency control and requires explicit planning for sequential vs. parallel phases and time estimates. Without them, the plan cannot optimise parallel execution or compute speedups.


e. worktree_isolation.concurrency.limit missing
The concurrency limit is null, yet concurrency control is a core v3.2 capability. The plan should set a maximum number of parallel steps or processes to respect resource constraints and avoid race conditions.


f. ground_truth_levels undefined
ground_truth_levels.level_definitions lists only placeholder commands and evidence; expect_coverage_min and required-by-phase/step maps are empty. The specification requires explicit verification levels with coverage targets to guarantee that outputs satisfy the required ground‑truth fidelity and metrics.


g. Missing step contracts (phase_contracts and step_contracts)
Most phases have empty steps arrays; only two steps (PH‑00/STEP‑001 and PH‑02/STEP‑001) are defined, leaving subsequent phases without contracts. Within the defined steps, numerous fields are blank: read‑only paths, rollback commands/evidence, idempotency flags, backup location, mutation guard policy, before/after hash paths, manual review reasons and runtime limits. The spec demands step‑level contracts, including idempotency and concurrency enforcement, backup requirements and explicit rollback procedures. Without step contracts, execution cannot be validated by gates such as GATE-CFG-003 through GATE-CFG-006.


h. Undefined validation_gates
Many gate entries have no associated step_id or phase, making them unresolvable. The technical spec lists all required gates and mandates that each step be bound to them.


i. Empty infrastructure.permissions
Filesystem write, network access and network allowlist permissions are null, and CI parity is unspecified. The instructions require declaring minimal infrastructure permissions and ensuring CI parity to prevent environment drift.


j. planning_hierarchy and field_extensions undefined
All module, component and execution‑packet levels are blank, as are the field extensions (implementation style, engine component ID, executor dependencies, etc.). The spec expects a planning hierarchy linking requirements to modules, components and execution packets to provide traceability and canonicalisation.


k. pipeline_boundary_contract missing
planning_stage_output, execution_stage_input and input_exclusivity_rule are empty. The instruction document requires explicitly defining what artifacts constitute the plan output, what inputs are allowed into execution, and the exclusivity rule for ensuring that only authorised inputs are consumed.


l. Incomplete mutation and intake models
mutation_model.workstream_decomposition_rules and independence_criteria are empty. The spec emphasises workstream isolation and independence to guarantee safe mutation of resources during execution. Similarly, the task_intake_model.sources and normalization_target are undefined, leaving the plan unclear on how tasks are taken in and normalised.


m. Missing traceability mappings and canonicalisation
The traceability section has all mappings (requirement‑to‑module, module‑to‑component, component‑to‑execution‑packet, gap‑to‑execution‑packet, requirement‑to‑pattern, component‑to‑pattern, step‑to‑executor, pattern‑to‑config‑schema, pattern‑to‑output‑contract) empty. The canonicalisation contract has no rules. The spec’s “canonicalization and traceability” guarantees require that each requirement, component and pattern can be traced through to executors and outputs. Without these mappings, there is no auditable lineage of behaviour, patterns or executors.


n. Missing execution_baseline.dependencies and scripts
The execution baseline lists only a placeholder commit hash and has no dependencies or scripts. The spec requires a baseline commit and explicit scripts to ensure reproducible environments and to support rollback.


o. Incomplete rollback_plan
The global rollback procedure is a placeholder and there is no failure_mode_to_recovery_mapping. Spec instructs that a rollback plan be prepared, mapping failure modes to recovery actions.


p. Undefined state_transitions.invalid_transitions
No forbidden state transitions are listed. In v3.2, concurrency control and gating rely on preventing invalid state transitions.


q. Sparse executor_registry and execution_patterns.task_pattern_mappings
Only two example executors are registered, and only two task kinds are mapped to patterns. The spec demands that all patterns be bound to approved executors and that tasks not execute ad hoc when a pattern exists. Without additional executors and mappings, most user tasks would trigger GATE-CFG-003 and halt the pipeline until pattern creation.


2. JSON patch mapping


The following shows where the current JSONs need patching. Each entry identifies the file/section and summarises the required fix.

File
JSON Path
Required Patch
Rationale
NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_AUTONOMOUS_DELIVERY_TEMPLATE_V3_2.json
task_analysis.* fields
Populate original_request, specificity_transformation.objective, methodology phases/parallel groups, success criteria, classification (complexity, domain, quality), inferred behaviour fields, decision log, pattern_resolution_outputs values.
The instruction document requires summarising the user request, classifying complexity/domain and logging the pattern resolution decision.
same
assumptions_scope arrays
List explicit assumptions, in‑scope and out‑of‑scope items, and define “definition of done.”
To uphold the “NO IMPLIED BEHAVIOUR” rule and ensure transparent scope.
same
file_manifest.global_files and entry_extensions
Populate with names of spec/config files, pattern packages and any plan artifacts; set pattern_id and pattern_role for each entry.
Configuration‑driven development requires referencing approved artifacts and patterns.
same
phase_plan.phase_overview, parallel_execution_groups and time_estimate fields
Describe each phase, identify which phases can run concurrently and estimate sequential/parallel duration and speedup.
The plan must explicitly schedule phases and calculate concurrency benefits.
same
worktree_isolation.concurrency.limit
Set a maximum number of concurrent threads/processes.
Concurrency control is a required v3.2 capability.
same
ground_truth_levels.level_definitions
Define commands/evidence for each ground‑truth level and set expect_coverage_min; fill required_by_phase and required_by_step maps.
To enforce verification targets and metrics.
same
phase_contracts and step_contracts
Add step lists for all phases and define per‑step contract fields: read‑only paths, rollback commands/evidence, idempotency flags, backup requirements, mutation guard policies, before/after hash paths, manual review reasons and runtime limits.
Each step must be governed by explicit contracts so validation gates can enforce pattern compliance.
same
validation_gates.*.step_id and phase
Map each gate to the phase and step it validates.
The technical spec defines gates but they must be bound to specific steps to be enforceable.
same
infrastructure.permissions and ci_parity
Specify which file system writes are allowed, whether network access is permitted and on which hosts; declare if CI parity is required.
To ensure minimal privileges and environment parity.
same
planning_hierarchy and field_extensions
Define module‑level and component‑level hierarchy and fill in the field extensions (implementation style, engine component ID, behaviour spec artifacts, config schema artifacts, executor dependencies, approved extension slots).
Traceability and canonicalisation demand a detailed planning hierarchy linking requirements to code and executors.
same
pipeline_boundary_contract
Set planning_stage_output to the path of the plan document, define execution_stage_input (e.g., the plan JSON and approved config), and specify an input_exclusivity_rule that blocks other inputs.
The instruction document requires explicit stage boundaries.
same
mutation_model & task_intake_model
Define workstream_decomposition_rules and independence_criteria to express how tasks can mutate resources safely; specify intake sources and normalization_target.
Necessary for concurrency control and safe resource mutation.
same
traceability & canonicalization_contract
Populate mappings from requirements to modules and patterns and from components to execution packets; fill gap_to_execution_packet, deferred_gaps, step_to_executor, pattern_to_config_schema, and pattern_to_output_contract and define canonicalisation rules.
To support auditability and ensure that all behaviours are traceable to specifications.
same
execution_baseline.dependencies and scripts
List the dependencies (e.g., Python packages or OS packages) and the baseline scripts used to reproduce the environment; update the placeholder commit to a real one.
Baseline reproducibility is required for rollback and consistency.
same
rollback_plan
Replace the placeholder global procedure with an actual step‑by‑step rollback plan and fill failure_mode_to_recovery_mapping for each failure mode.
The spec insists on a prepared rollback mapping for safe recovery.
same
state_transitions.invalid_transitions
Define which transitions between plan/step states are forbidden to prevent illegal concurrency or skipping of gates.
Supports concurrency control and gate enforcement.
same
executor_registry
Register additional executors for pattern creation, indexing, configuration updates, concurrency control and rollback tasks; ensure each executor is associated with supported patterns and input/output contracts.
The instruction doc emphasises an executor registry and prohibits freehand scripts; adding executors is essential when new patterns appear.
same
execution_patterns.task_pattern_mappings
Map every task kind used in the plan to a valid pattern; if a pattern does not exist, add a pattern_creation task or a placeholder indicating that new pattern development is required.
Pattern‑first execution forbids running a task without a registered pattern.

Conclusion


The v3.2 package provides the governance framework and indexing infrastructure for a pattern‑first, configuration‑driven, auditable execution pipeline. However, the provided JSON template remains largely skeletal: it lacks essential data in almost every section—task analysis, assumptions, phase/step contracts, concurrency settings, traceability mappings, baseline scripts, rollback procedures and more. To align with the instruction document and technical specification, the template must be populated with concrete values, additional executors and patterns must be registered, and missing fields must be filled or replaced with new structures as outlined above.

Explaining the purpose and usage of NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES.md


The NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES.md is a session manifest, not the source of truth. It serves as a roadmap and index. To use it: start with root JSONs, follow runbook inputs for changes, verify with blocker evidence, use the indexing tool to locate specific sections, and check readiness before patching. I'll cite the NPP file.


Thought for 37s

It is a session manifest.


It tells you, in one place, what each file in this v3.2 migration/indexing session is for, which files are authoritative, which ones are runbooks, which ones are tooling, and which ones are just evidence or deferred context. Its job is to stop you from guessing what a file does.


NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES


What it tells you:


The three root V3_2 JSONs are the core governance set:
technical spec = authoritative framework definition

template = executable plan structure

instruction doc = AI-facing rules for filling the template
NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES

The guardrails_template_project/* files are migration authority and runbook inputs, not the main source-of-truth JSONs. They tell you how the upgrade/migration was supposed to be executed.
NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES

index_generator.py, resolver.py, inventory.json, and the indexes/* files are the indexing/navigation toolchain for the three governance JSONs. They exist so you can resolve semantic IDs like GATE-003 or PH-00/STEP-001 to exact JSON Pointer locations instead of hunting manually.
NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES

The evidence/blocker_* files and evidence/PH-* files are proof artifacts showing what was fixed and whether indexing/readiness checks passed.
NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES

The file also tells you the role change that happened: the three root JSONs became the tracked authoritative working set, the runbooks became explicit execution authority, and the indexing toolchain now understands post-governance IDs like PAT-*, EXEC-*, and GATE-CFG-*.
NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES


How to use it:


Use it as a map before touching anything.
If you want to change planning rules, start with the three root JSONs, not the evidence files and not the deferred patch plans.
NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES

Use it to classify a file before acting on it.
Ask:
Is this file authoritative?

Is it an execution/runbook input?

Is it tooling?

Is it evidence only?

Is it deferred and intentionally not applied?
The file answers that directly. For example, the two v3_3_patch_plan_machine_* files are explicitly marked as deferred reference, not applied migration authority.
NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES

Use it to choose the right starting file.
Changing framework meaning or semantics → NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_TECHNICAL_SPECIFICATION_V3_2.json

Changing plan structure/fields/examples → NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_AUTONOMOUS_DELIVERY_TEMPLATE_V3_2.json

Changing AI fill rules/constraints → NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_INSTRUCTION_DOCUMENT_V3_2.json
NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES

Use it to trace supporting machinery.
If you need exact JSON locations, use index_generator.py and resolver.py. The file tells you those are the tools for semantic indexing and JSON Pointer resolution.
NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES

Use it to verify current state before planning more changes.
Check the blocker-remediation and readiness evidence files it lists. That tells you whether you are building on a fixed/indexed baseline or reopening already-resolved issues.
NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES


How not to use it:


Do not treat it as the place where rules live.

Do not patch this file first when you want behavioral changes.

Do not use it as proof that a change is required by itself.


It is a navigation/control document, not the normative spec.


The practical workflow is:


Read NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES.md first to classify the file set.
NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES

Use the instruction doc to know how fields are supposed to be filled and constrained. The instruction doc explicitly says the template is the canonical contract and that pattern-first/configuration-driven execution rules apply.

Use the technical spec to understand what each top-level section means and how it is supposed to behave.

NPP_SESSION_FILE_PURPOSES


Bluntly: this file answers “what is this file and why does it exist?” for the whole session. Use it to orient yourself, pick the real authority file, and avoid editing the wrong artifact.

Thought for 20s


Use this split:


1. Enforcement process


The current v3.2 model is a fail-closed enforcement pipeline, not just documentation. The process is:


Classify the task

Search the pattern registry

Bind the task to an approved pattern

If no pattern exists, create a PATTERN_CREATION path first

Validate pattern completeness, executor binding, config/schema surfaces, and behavior-binding proof

Only then allow implementation/execution

Run validation gates, collect evidence, update run status and mutation ledger

Detect anti-patterns and halt or self-heal when required
NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_INSTRUCTION…
NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_TECHNICAL_S…


That is the core enforcement loop. The instruction doc is explicit: no ad hoc execution, no blank pattern_id, no freehand implementation when a pattern is missing, and no behavior sourced from prompt prose instead of approved spec/config artifacts.

NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_INSTRUCTION…

3. What actually enforces the system


Bluntly, enforcement is not one file. It is the combination of:

4. What is still missing if you want hard enforcement in practice


The docs now define the model pretty well, but hard enforcement in a running system still depends on whether these runtime pieces actually exist and are wired:


real validator scripts for each GATE-CFG-*

real PATTERN_INDEX.yaml

real executor registry

real pattern package schemas

real gate runner / result schemas

real audit + ledger validation

real anti-pattern detectors that halt execution instead of just describing risk
NEWPHASEPLANPROCESS_TECHNICAL_S…
Error Taxonomy Across Historica…


That is the difference between documented enforcement and operational enforcement.


5. Practical way to think about it


The enforcement stack is:


policy
→ invariants, instructions, spec


admission control
→ pattern resolution, executor registration, gate checks


execution control
→ worktree isolation, file scope, protected paths, runtime limits


proof
→ evidence bundles, behavior-binding proof, metrics, audit trail, mutation ledger


post-run integrity
→ anti-pattern detection, run status coherence, ledger validation, rollback completeness


If you want, the next useful step is to turn this into a concrete enforcement artifact inventory with three columns:


artifact | enforcement purpose | required runtime implementation
